sections.current_situation:
The Reimagining Public Safety (RPS) initiative is a Strategic Plan Priority Project,
advancing Berkeley's goal of creating a resilient, safe, connected, and prepared city.
This initiative is being implemented in three distinct phases, focusing on equitable and
community-centered public safety transformation:
Phase 1 (2020-2022): Community Process and Research
On July 14, 2020, the City Council adopted an omnibus package to reimagine public
safety and policing in Berkeley. This phase focused on planning, analysis, and early
program development. Activities included:
 Community/Consultant Engagement: Partnering with experts to develop an
equitable, community-focused safety model.
 Specialized Care Unit (SCU) Development: Initiating a pilot program to
reassign non-criminal police service calls to a Specialized Care Unit.
Page 3
Page 4 of 301
Reimagining Public Safety Status Report INFORMATION CALENDAR
July 22, 2025
 Community Crisis Response (CCR) Bridge Services: Providing interim crisis
support services while the SCU program was being developed.
 Priority Dispatch: Developing dispatch models to route specific calls to
alternative responders, including the possibility of transitioning some functions to
the Fire Department or other entities.
 City Auditor Review: Conducting an analysis of emergency call data and the
police budget.
 Fair and Impartial Policing: Implementing policies to ensure fairness and equity
in policing.
 BerkDOT Development: Planning for the creation of a Berkeley Department of
Transportation (BerkDOT) to handle traffic enforcement with a focus on racial
justice.
 Violence Intervention Program: Expanding violence prevention efforts through
targeted programs.
Following the adoption of the omnibus package, a multi-departmental working group
was formed to guide implementation, including representatives from the City Manager's
Office, Police, Fire, HHCS, Public Works, and the City Attorney’s Office.
In January 2021, the Reimagining Public Safety
sections.background:
The City of Berkeley imposes a range of fines and fees on individuals for various violations
and services. Although intended to maintain public order and offset administrative costs,
studies show that such charges may place a disproportionate burden on low-income
residents. Research indicates that relatively minor fees can lead to substantial financial
hardship, as individuals with limited resources may need to choose between paying fines
and covering basic necessities such as food, rent, or utilities.
9
Page 93 of 301
A report by SPUR in 2021 highlighted that fines and fees disproportionately harm
low-income Californians, pushing many families into debt and impeding their ability to
meet basic needs.17 The report noted that 48 percent of individuals grappling with fines
and/or fees could not pay their other monthly bills, 40 percent had to skip paying utility
bills, and 37 percent could not afford groceries.18 These findings underscore the significant
burden that fines and fees can impose on low-income individuals, often leading to difficult
choices between fulfilling legal obligations and meeting essential living expenses.
17 Denney, Jacob. “More Harm Than Good: Building a More Just Fine and Fee System in California,” June 2021.
https://www.spur.org/sites/default/files/2021-06/SPUR_More_Harm_Than_Good_0.pdf
18 Ibid.
10
Page 94 of 301
Data at the California Budget and Policy Center shows that in 2023, 63 percent of
households making less than $35,000 per year, 46 percent making $35,000-$74,999, and 30
percent making $75,000-$149,999 per year, reported difficulty paying for basic expenses.19
Households living paycheck-to-paycheck can be highly vulnerable to unanticipated
expenses, as their budgets are already stretched thin by necessities like rent, food, and
transportation. The lack of disposable income and emergency savings means that a
significant portion of Californians, not just those who fall under the “low-income” threshold,
are at risk of falling into deeper
sections.recommendation:
The data presented in this report may help the City of Berkeley provide behavioral health and
homelessness services that meet the needs of community members without relying on law
enforcement to address social service and health needs. These recommendations were
derived from the conclusions by identifying key areas of intersection and overlap and were
discussed with City staff from multiple departments to identify the utility and feasibility of
recommendations.
46
Page 264 of 301
Recomme ndation #1
Use mental health services and crisis response data – not CAD -
when assessing the volume and characteristics of mental health,
substance use, and/or homelessness needs.
There are substantial limitations to the data collected in CAD that preclude it from being the
sole source of information to assess the community’s mental health needs. These limitations,
exacerbated by the nature of the quickly evolving crisis response environment, mean that there
are too many potential interpretations of CAD data to be able to use CAD for a behavioral
health landscape assessment. Nor are specific behavioral health characteristics identifiable
through CAD data.
Improving CAD to a degree that would make such an assessment feasible with this system
alone (e.g., updates to call types and call type assignment practices, narrative notetaking
protocols, disposition assignment), would require substantial City resources. Additionally, once
those improvements were made, it would take time – likely several years – before those
changes would be reflected in the data. Moreover, some limitations cannot be addressed, such
as different interpretations between callers, dispatchers, and police officers of what constitutes
a mental health related event.
Additionally, if changes were made to Dispatch’s call-taking or call-routing procedures but
police were still the only available response team for those calls, then the adoption of new
protocols would likely be low and/or inconsistent. CAD was designed for
sections.preamble:
Page 1 of 301
Office of the City Manager
INFORMATION CALENDAR
July 22, 2025
To: Honorable Mayor and Members of the City Council
From: Paul Buddenhagen, City Manager
Submitted by: Carianna Arredondo, Assistant to the City Manager, City Manager’s Office
Subject: Reimagining Public Safety Status Report
SUMMARY
The Reimagining Public Safety (RPS) initiative is currently in Phase 3 (2024–2026):
Continued Implementation and Expansion. This phase focuses on sustaining key
reforms, enhancing service coordination, and transitioning pilot programs into
integrated, long-term systems. It builds on earlier efforts to establish an equitable,
effective, and community-responsive public safety framework, emphasizing long-range
planning, strategic resource allocation, and data-informed decision-making.
This summary provides a high-level overview of recent developments and does not
capture the full scope of work underway across departments. A comprehensive update
on current efforts, accomplishments, and next steps is included in the Summer 2025
Reimagining Public Safety Status Update and companion appendix. Together, these
materials reflect the City’s ongoing commitment to a responsive, inclusive, and well-
coordinated approach to safety and well-being in Berkeley.
Since the last update in October 2024, the City has achieved several milestones:
 Gun Violence Prevention: The City continued to expand implementation of the
Gun Violence Intervention and Prevention Program (GVIPP), a core strategy
within the RPS framework. In partnership with Live Free USA and the National
Institute for Criminal Justice Reform (NICJR), the City launched the Community
Violence Intervention (CVI) Collaborative in December 2024. The Collaborative
meets monthly to strengthen implementation, improve coordination, and inform
strategy across partners. Staff also joined a regional CVI partnership in February
2025 to align strategies with neighboring jurisdictions. Since launch, GVIPP has
served more than 500 indi
